# Transcrição: 03-Account Attribution Analysis

- **Fonte:** 03-Account Attribution Analysis
- **Duração:** 16:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:04]

Now when we're looking at attribution, one of the things to keep an eye on is not just your contribution paths. So you know, someone clicked on this ad or someone clicked in this marketing channel and kind of what that looked like. The other side of attribution is what happens after that click and how long it takes, for example, for someone to buy. This sheet here is something that we use to analyze what that path looks like. In particular, once I understand on average what the attribution looks like after the first click, I can then determine what my retargeting funnels look like. So in this ad account here, and I wanted to share this data with you in this template because the attribution is quite active. So we call it active attribution. If there are cells that are generated on a regular basis, many days after the first click is generated. So what I want to illustrate here before I show you how this is set up is I'm looking at revenue and sales uplift. So you can look at one or both columns because they will be roughly similar. So one is the actual transactions, the other one is revenue. So the columns here are driven by average all of value. And this is just the absolute number of transactions. So if I look at the revenue side of things first, firstly, the way Facebook tracks your attribution is based on click and view data. By default, ads manager is set to show you data for any transactions or signups or registrations that have happened one day after someone's viewed an ad, whether they've clicked or not. And then up to 28 days after they've clicked an ad. That's the default attribution. You can change that for your ad account. You can also change that in ads manager in terms of the review or the kind of views that you're looking at in ads manager. And you can also look at it separately in ads manager as well. Now I pulled it into sheets through Supermetrics and I'll show you how I'm kind of looking at the data there as well. And what I've done is to split the data for one day click, seven day click and 28 day click. So what that means is I'm only looking at data, for example, for sales or revenue that generated after one day click and one day click only. So what I'm saying there is if a purchase happens tomorrow from today's click, don't count that. I just want to see sales that are generated from today's clicks. Then if I look at seven day attribution, and this is seven day click, all I want to know is sales that have been generated within seven days of that first click. So if someone clicks today and buys tomorrow, show me the sales. If they click today buying six days time, show me if they click and buying eight days time, I don't want to see it. Then I look at the full 28 days and I'm looking at where what the comparison looks like all

## [03:04 - 06:04]

the way through and I'm looking at a 28 day view because after that there's no more data. Facebook doesn't by default and right now track beyond 28 days click. So if I track this down, what I've got going is what uplift am I seeing on the attribution models versus the one day click. So here I can already see that the seven day click has 10% more revenue shown than the one day click and the 28 day is the same because it's the same time period. So as we go through and the seven day ends, so we go about here, what we're actually seeing is that the 28 day conversion window is starting to kick in and show more data. So this seven day is rolling. So you have a seven day rolling period. So for example, clicks here, you have a seven day roll back there and then you have seven days here all the way down. So you've got seven day windows all the way through. But what's interesting is if you go all the way through and look at 28 day click analysis, what this actually shows is it will show sales that happened in the seven days and it will also add on sales that happened beyond that seven days as well. So you start to see those percentages start to creep up on certain days all the way up and you've got this day here, for example, which shows that compared to the one day click view, there were more than double the sales that were recorded over 28 days click. So that means there was a huge number of people that had clicked an ad but had actually purchased at a later period all the way back on that attribution window as well. And what this tells me is there is a super active attribution window going on that sales are still racking up way beyond that kind of first click period. So as I'm looking down, for example, all the way down on that 28 day click, we're generating a big number of a huge revenue beyond that purchase that we probably see it in Google Analytics or whatever it is. So this, for example, is showing me that there are still a big number of sales coming into that seven day and 28 day window. That gives me an indicator into how to set up my retargeting. So when you look at retargeting, a lot of people will say you should always do seven days and maybe do 14. Some people say do 30, some people 21. You can use data to determine what that should be like. So this is the revenue view. This is the sales view. This is in particular looking at return on ad spend. So for example, if I just scroll to the side, you can see the days here. So this is in UK format. This is the 6th of May, and this is the 9th of April. So if we go all the way back to 9th of April, if we used a one day click attribution, and that's all we were tracking, we would have had a ROAS return on ad spend of 1.2, which

## [06:04 - 09:08]

meant for every dollar we spent, we got $1.20 back. If we use a seven day attribution window, we would have had a ROAS of 1.9. If we use the full 28 days that Facebook gives us, then we would have showed a 2.8 return on ad spend, which means for every dollar spent, we got $2.80 back. And that's a big difference. And that makes a big difference in your analysis because on that day, on that one day, for example, if that's all you were tracking on that one day, we may have made different budgeting, growth, scaling, and optimization decisions. And if we were using the 28 day window, and we knew that conversions were going to come in, so here's an example for you. On this particular ad account, on any given day, when the return on ad spend is 1.2 to 1.4, or 120% to 140%, on that particular day, we know that we're in a good space from the Facebook ads point of view because 28 days later, that attribution generally more than doubles because of the sales that are happening later on. Now, this particular store, it's an average order value of about $100. So we know based on the product and the niche and how the market responds that most people need time to make that decision. So what would you do in that scenario? Number one, you would want to know based on performance on that day, top of funnel, and kind of all the way through, what does good look like? So for us, account-wide, good looks like 1.2 to 1.4 ROAS, and we split that into our four funnel systems so we know what good looks like all the way through the funnel. Secondly, it helps to dictate our retargeting windows. So initially, this ad account had smaller retargeting windows. We actually merged them up to 30 days and simplified the whole thing. And we found that return on ad spend and CPA stabilized and performed far better at that 30 day retargeting window because of the data we're seeing here. And that helps us to decide also, what do we do in that time? So our retargeting messages are combined with email. So email capture plays a big part on this store as well, to try and keep the product and the brand front of mind because we know that they're going to convert at some point within the next seven, 14, or 28 days because the data tells us that as well. So here you can see, for example, in kind of hard numbers format, what that looks like. So what I've done here is I'm looking at on day one. So if I'm looking at this particular day here, which has the full data, what revenue was driven on the different views, obviously for one day, it's all the same. And there's no attribution that can kick in because there's no time passed. If I look at the seven days elapsed from this kind of the latest period, on a one day click, how much revenue was generated on a seven day and a 28 day,

## [09:08 - 12:09]

these are the same because the windows, it's important to know that the seven day click, 28 day click includes that seven days. So the numbers are the same. But here's the thing, in that seven days, we're almost doubling, in fact, we're more than doubling our revenue. So if we were just looking at our stats on a daily basis, we would be blinded by the fact that today's revenue doesn't look good. But I know in seven days time, we're almost gonna more than double that. And then you look at the 14 day trend, and then you look at the 21 day trend, and you look at 28 day. What I can see from this is my ideal, if I look at my average or mean time to convert, it's somewhere between seven and 14 days. That most people will end up converting. And that's really important data. So I know what kind of messages to maybe start testing, not just on Facebook accounts, but also email, and maybe even messenger, if you're pushing that as well. So it's a really important part of understanding your customer and how they interact as well. And then here I'm just looking at the revenue uplifts based on the data. This was a promotional period, so that's a slightly odd one for this. But at least I can see that there is something out of the norm. But I can see, for example, my seven day click conversion window, on average, gives me a 30 to 35% uplift on sales. And then my 28 day starts to pick up, and I can see, I'm almost seeing like 54% uplift on sales over that 28 days. So that's an important indication for me, because although Facebook isn't tracking, maybe not tracking those sales. I know Facebook's contributing. So whether the Facebook's getting the last click or not, and probably not, if you're running email and Google search and organic channels and other things like that. So it's important to know what the outcomes are from that click, and what's happening after it as well. Then in terms of the data setup, again, I'm pulling this through from Supermetrics. Now what I do for this particular query so I can actually bring data for all the different click views is I run three separate queries based on just segmenting the click value. So one day click, seven day click, and 28 day click. So right now you can't run those as one query, but that's fine. I run three queries and just refresh them when I need the data. So it's really super simple to set up. You select your data source, your ad account, you select your date. So I've done the last 28 days. And then in terms of a metric, I'm only going after the amount spent, the conversion value, so the revenue, and how many purchases were made. Now if purchases and revenue and things like that are not important to you, you can easily swap them out. So although you might need to change the headings to suit, you can just swap them out. So for example, if website purchases is not your biggest metric and

## [12:09 - 15:09]

maybe it's registrations or something else, just swap that out and you can analyze the data for that as well. And then I'm splitting by date, so I can see that the 28 days split by date as well. No filters. And then when I come into the options, I'm selecting the window. So here I've got one day click. I would put the cursor onto here. I would run my one day click and then I would fill out that data. Once I'm done, I would then take that same query and do it again and change it to seven day click, etc, etc. It actually is a further build from that. You can test different models here as well. So you could do one day click, one day view, one day click, seven day view, etc. You can also do view data on its own. So I look at click because that's what I'm more interested in. But you could compare models of view as well. So if that sounds too complex and it's not something you're interested in, that's fine. If you're a bit more advanced and you want to take this further, then you can actually build on that and take that into that level of analysis as well. So I use Supermetrics because it's a super quick tool to pull that data through. This is something that we would pull in on refresh once a day, for example. We would be tracking this maybe once or twice a month. It's not something that generally would be coming in too frequently to look at, unlike some of our other dashboards. But it's something to keep an eye on and something to help us realign, readjust, looking at promotions, looking at maybe changes in our marketing to see how they're reacting to contribution for attribution. And then finally here, I have a graph that's based on this data and it's illustrating the impact of attribution. So here is the current dates and this is the 28-day period underneath that. And the blue line is my one-day click revenue. The red line is my seven-day click revenue and the 28-day is yellow. And you can see the differences in the revenue numbers and what's coming through and where the trends start to increase as well. So the seven-day click revenue starts to increase around day four and five and then it really kicks in. So I would say looking at this data, two to three days is about the right decision time for this style of campaign. For example, maybe you split out people that visit the site and maybe product page within day zero to three and maybe you're less aggressive on your products and you're working more on your messaging and brand. What I mean by that is it's fairly normal to assume that as soon as someone's visited your site, you need to absolutely hammer them with retargeting ads, convincing them to buy. But are you considering the fact that someone's still thinking about the product? Is telling them to buy now and get a discount and things like that the absolutely right thing to do?

## [15:09 - 16:02]

Maybe not. Maybe they're price comparing with you and a competitor or maybe they're just not sure if they can trust you. Maybe your brand isn't that well known or whatever it is. Maybe the first two, three, four days need to be handled differently in your ads and in your emails to be enforceable on the sales because the data would suggest, especially if you start to see this as a trend, that people are taking two to three days naturally before they buy. So you can maybe support that and try different messages earlier on and then start pushing more hard of sales messages. Or you might look at this and say, well, maybe I can be more aggressive in the first few days and get those sales in. Maybe I can shift that behavior. So this is data that I would use to help me understand my retargeting, what kind of strategies I should put in place and how I need to set up my audiences and the messages and start to test within that as well.
